Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a proper study routine most of the time. I'd go through phases where I'd binge-watch lecture videos for hours, then not touch anything for weeks. With work and other stuff going on, I just grabbed study time whenever I could find it. I'm more of a visual learner, so I'd focus heavily on diagrams and video content when I did sit down to study, which helped me absorb things quickly even in short bursts.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, definitely driven by deadlines more than any schedule. Some weeks I'd barely look at course materials, then I'd have these intense cramming sessions right before something was due. Being 26 and juggling limited resources, I had to fit studying around everything else in my life. It wasn't ideal, but I made it work when it really counted.
Q3: Usually it was a deadline looming or realizing I'd missed something important that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get an email reminder about an assignment and that would be my cue. I rarely logged in just to browse or stay on top of things proactively. It was more reactive than anything else.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a habit for me - I logged in maybe once a week at most. I kept meaning to make it more regular, but life kept getting in the way. The few times I did get into a rhythm, something would come up and break it, and I'd have to start over.
Q5: The video lectures were by far the most useful for me since I learn best visually. I could watch them at my own pace, pause, rewind, and really absorb the diagrams and demonstrations. I also found the discussion posts helpful when I actually engaged with them - seeing how other students approached problems gave me different perspectives. Those 114 minutes of video watching were probably my most productive learning time.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary texts. With limited resources, I couldn't always access everything, and honestly, walls of text just don't stick with me the way visuals do. I also skipped most optional quizzes and practice exercises - they felt like extra work when I was already stretched thin. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later rarely came.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the exams or assignments. If something was going to help me understand a concept I'd be tested on, I was in. Visual content that explained things clearly was always worth it. Anything that felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to my grade usually got pushed aside.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required stuff had to get done no matter what, so it got prioritized. Optional things, even if they might have been helpful, always fell to the bottom of the list. There's only so many hours in the day, and when you're working with limited resources and time, you have to make tough choices about what actually matters.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of lecture slides and videos early on and studied from those offline constantly. I'd screenshot diagrams and review them on my phone during breaks at work. I also used YouTube videos and free resources online to supplement what I was learning. A lot of my actual studying happened completely outside the LMS.
Q10: There were tons of times like that. I'd print out slides and study them at home or at work during downtime. I'd discuss concepts with a friend who was taking similar courses, working through problems together. Sometimes I'd spend hours reviewing downloaded materials or watching related videos on YouTube. None of that showed up anywhere in the system, but it was real learning.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture video once, then watch it three or four more times offline over the following days. Same with PDFs and slides - one download, multiple study sessions. My internet isn't always reliable, so downloading and working offline just made more sense for my situation. The LMS would only show that initial access, not all the work that came after.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough. I was dealing with some financial stress and had to pick up extra shifts at work. My attendance dropped significantly during that period, and I fell behind on assignments. The anxiety built up, and it became this cycle where the more I fell behind, the harder it was to face logging in and seeing everything I'd missed. The CBT sessions actually helped me work through some of that.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the last thing I wanted to do was sit in front of a computer navigating the LMS. Fatigue made me less likely to engage with anything that required effort. I'd sometimes open the platform, stare at it for a few minutes, then close it because I just couldn't focus. Competing responsibilities were constantly pulling me away from coursework.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have videos playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be thinking about bills or work or just zoning out from exhaustion. I might technically be logged in and watching something, but I wasn't actually absorbing anything. Those sessions felt like wasted time, but I kept hoping something would stick.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed - when you're behind, starting feels impossible. Part of it was prioritizing immediate concerns over future deadlines. I'd tell myself I worked better under pressure anyway, which was sometimes true but also sometimes just an excuse. The CBT sessions helped me recognize some of these avoidance patterns.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. Without them, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all some weeks. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the energy to binge through materials and submit something. It wasn't healthy, but the pressure of a deadline cut through all the other noise and forced me to act. My response times averaged over 17 hours, which shows I wasn't exactly proactive.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it motivated me to keep going - it felt like proof that my approach was working despite the chaos. Bad feedback sometimes made me want to disengage more, like why bother if I'm going to struggle anyway. But overall, knowing I was doing well on exams gave me confidence that my unconventional study methods were actually effective. That positive reinforcement helped me push through.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and challenging for me. Having clear deadlines gave me the pressure I needed to act, but the pace assumed more consistent engagement than I could give. The flexibility of online courses was essential for my situation, but it also made it easy to drift. I appreciated that I could catch up in bursts rather than needing to be present constantly.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because it matched my learning style. Having materials available for download was crucial given my internet situation. But the sheer number of courses - I was enrolled in 20 - made it hard to give proper attention to any single one. The discussion requirements felt forced sometimes, though I did find value in peer interactions when I actually participated.
Q20: I think those students might be searching for something - maybe they're confused and clicking around trying to understand what they need to do. Or they could be dealing with anxiety or perfectionism, reviewing materials obsessively but freezing when it comes to actually submitting work. Sometimes high activity is just spinning wheels without traction. It doesn't necessarily mean productive engagement.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. My LMS activity was low - I barely logged in - but I still got a distinction. I think it shows that engagement happens in many forms, not just clicking around a platform. I was deeply engaged with the material, just not in ways the system could track. Efficient studying offline can absolutely lead to strong performance without high online activity.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns - when people are active, what they click on, how long they spend. But they miss all the offline work, the mental processing, the discussions with friends, the real understanding that happens away from the screen. They also can't tell the difference between someone genuinely engaged and someone just clicking through mindlessly. The numbers tell a very incomplete story.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when you're thinking about concepts even when you're not studying, making connections, wanting to know more. For me, engagement happened in bursts - intense periods of real focus and learning, even if they weren't frequent. It's about quality of attention, not quantity of logins.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually learned and retained despite my irregular patterns. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with downloaded materials, the conversations with peers outside the platform, or the mental work of processing concepts during my commute. It also wouldn't show my resilience - how I kept coming back and performing well despite medium stress and all the obstacles in my life. My distinction grade came from real learning, just not the kind that leaves digital footprints.
